title: CIA: Prince David Peter Abashidze

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: CIA: Prince David Peter Abashidze
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I hereby request copies of all records in the custody or control of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) — and any predecessor agency, including the Office of Strategic Services (OSS) — that relate to, mention, reference, or were generated in connection with the individual described below.

Subject of Request -

Full Name: David Peter Abashidze
Also known as / name variants: Davit Abashidze; D.P. Abashidze; Prince Abashidze; Давид Петрович Абашидзе (Cyrillic)
Title: Prince (Knyaz), of the Georgian noble House of Abashidze
Date of Birth: July 17, 1878
Place of Birth: Tiflis (Tbilisi), Georgia, Imperial Russian Empire
Date of Death: July 11, 1952
Place of Death: New York City, New York, United States
Nationality at Death: Naturalized United States citizen
Business Affiliation: Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation (New York)
Proof of death is enclosed: a copy of the obituary published in The New York Times on July 12, 1952, confirming the subject's death and the above biographical details.
Link to obit: https://www.nytimes.com/1952/07/12/archives/david-p-abasifidzu-a-georgian-prince.html

Records Sought - 

I request all responsive records regardless of format (paper, microfilm, electronic, index cards, or any other medium), including but not limited to:

Any name-indexed files, subject files, or personality files concerning David Peter Abashidze or the Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation;
Any records generated or received during the period 1940–1952 relating to Mr. Abashidze's business dealings with Soviet state entities or Soviet-affiliated trading partners, including cables, memoranda, reports, assessments, or contact reports;
Any records generated or received during the OSS period (1941–1945) referencing Mr. Abashidze, whether in the context of émigré intelligence, Caucasian affairs, or Soviet commercial penetration;
Any records referencing Mr. Abashidze's multilingual capabilities (he was known to speak Georgian, Russian, and English) or his potential utility as a source, contact, or liaison;
Any records relating to the House of Abashidze or Georgian émigré networks in New York City in which David Peter Abashidze is named or can be reasonably identified;
Any index entries, card files, or database entries in the CIA's Central Reference and Records Facility (CRRF) or any predecessor index system referencing the above name variants or the Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation;
Any liaison referrals or records shared with, received from, or jointly generated with the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the State Department, the Office of Naval Intelligence, or Military Intelligence (G-2) concerning the above subject.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware

title: FBI: Prince David Peter Abashidze

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FBI: Prince David Peter Abashidze
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I hereby request all records maintained by the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) pertaining to the following individual:

Full Name: David Peter Abashidze 
Title: Prince
Date of Birth: July 17th 1878 - Tbilisi, Georgia (Imperial Russian Empire) 
Date of Death: July 11 1952 - New York City 

Proof of death: David Peter Abashidze's death was widely reported and his obituary appeared in the July 12th 1953 edition of the New York Times. Abashidze was the owner of the "Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation" at the time of this death. He was a rare, private U.S. citizen who had business contracts with the Soviet state. 

Link to obit: https://www.nytimes.com/1952/07/12/archives/david-p-abasifidzu-a-georgian-prince.html

I am requesting a search of the Central Records System (CRS) for both "main files" and "cross-reference" files. This request specifically includes, but is not limited to, correspondence, internal memoranda, surveillance logs, and investigative reports where Abashidze is the subject or is mentioned in relation to other individuals. Please conduct a search for Abashidze records from any and all FBI offices, including HQ, field offices, legal attaché offices worldwide, and facilities that maintain records under the FBI’s control.

Please conduct a search for any correspondence (either to or from) Abashidze and the following bureau officials:

J. Edgar Hoover
Clyde Tolson
Edward Tamm
D. Milton Ladd 

According to a blog post read, it is important that I state the following: "As you know, the Federal Bureau of Investigation is required to search the records stored at NARA’s Washington Records Center in Suitland, MD, but still technically under the FBI’s control. For more information see 36 CFR 1250.8" Please also conduct a physical search of records at NARA's Washington Records Center in Suitland, for any records or documents related to David Peter Abashidze. 

If the bureau cannot fulfill certain parts of this request or certain portions must be redacted please provide any reasonable segregable portion of a requested record after removing or redacting those portions claimed to be exempt, explain in writing the justification for redacting the record, indicate the extent of any redactions on the portion of the record which is made available or published and where technically feasible indicate on the redacted portion itself the specific exemption(s) claimed.

Thank you to the bureau for the commitment to transparency.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware

title: MFCU abuse / neglect referrals and outcomes

communications.subject: Nevada Public Records Act Request: MFCU abuse / neglect referrals and outcomes
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Nevada Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

for the period January 1, 2019 through the date this request is processed: 

1. Aggregate complaint, referral, and case data

Records sufficient to show, by calendar year:

the number of complaints or referrals received by the Medicaid Fraud Control Unit involving alleged patient abuse or neglect;
the number of such complaints or referrals involving community-based, residential, group-home, board-and-care, assisted-living, provider-operated, or other noninstitutional congregate settings;
the source of referral, including but not limited to Adult Protective Services, Nevada Medicaid, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, law enforcement, facility/provider self-reports, family members, advocates, or other sources;
the allegation type, including abuse, neglect, exploitation, sexual abuse, physical abuse, emotional abuse, medication-related neglect, restraint, seclusion, unexplained injury, serious bodily injury, hospitalization, death, elopement, theft, or similar categories if tracked;
the number of matters opened for investigation;
the number of matters declined, closed without action, referred elsewhere, resolved civilly, referred for criminal prosecution, or resulting in any other enforcement or administrative outcome;
the number of matters referred by the Attorney General or MFCU to Nevada Medicaid, Adult Protective Services, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, local law enforcement, or local prosecutors.

2. Logs, spreadsheets, dashboards, or reports

Any logs, spreadsheets, dashboards, annual reports, quarterly reports, summaries, or other records sufficient to show the information requested in Item 1.

3. Policies, manuals, intake criteria, and jurisdiction guidance

All policies, manuals, written guidance, training materials, intake criteria, routing criteria, or jurisdictional guidance concerning:

when allegations of abuse or neglect fall within MFCU jurisdiction;
how MFCU distinguishes between Medicaid fraud matters and patient abuse/neglect matters;
how MFCU handles complaints involving community placements, residential facilities, provider-operated homes, or other noninstitutional settings;
referral protocols between the Attorney General/MFCU and Adult Protective Services, Nevada Medicaid, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, or local law enforcement.

4. Interagency agreements and referral protocols

Any memoranda of understanding, desk procedures, written referral protocols, or similar records governing coordination between the Attorney General or MFCU and:

Adult Protective Services;
Nevada Medicaid;
the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance;
local police or sheriffs’ offices;
district attorneys or local prosecutors.

5. Interagency communications about abuse/neglect in community placements

All emails, email attachments, memoranda, slide decks, meeting agendas, meeting minutes, or internal summaries from January 1, 2021 to present concerning abuse, neglect, exploitation, serious incidents, or referral failures in community-based or residential care settings, where the communication includes any of the following terms:

“group home”
“community placement”
“board and care”
“assisted living”
“HCBS”
“abuse”
“neglect”
“exploitation”
“critical incident”
“restraint”
“seclusion”
“medication error”
“death”
“serious injury”
“referral”
“APS”
“HCQC”
“Nevada Medicaid”

To narrow this request, I am seeking communications between or among the Attorney General’s Office / MFCU and other public agencies, not all internal emails on unrelated matters.

6. Closed case summaries and outcome records

Any de-identified case summaries, closing summaries, synopsis records, or other records sufficient to show the nature and outcome of closed MFCU matters involving allegations of abuse or neglect in community-based or residential settings, excluding personally identifying patient information.

7. Public-facing or releasable enforcement materials

Any press releases, public summaries, case announcements, settlement materials, prosecution summaries, or other releasable records concerning closed abuse/neglect matters involving residential or community care settings during the requested period.

8. Top provider/facility records

Records sufficient to show the providers or facilities most frequently appearing in complaints, referrals, or investigations handled by MFCU concerning abuse or neglect in community-based or residential settings during the requested period, including the total number of appearances for each provider or facility if tracked.

If full records cannot be produced for any item above, please provide records sufficient to show the requested information in aggregate or de-identified form. I am not requesting protected health information or personal identifying information of alleged victims.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

David Cutie

title: Public Records Request — Denver Generative AI Records

communications.subject: Colorado Open Records Act Request: Public Records Request — Denver Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Records Administrator
City and County of Denver
201 W. Colfax Avenue
Denver, CO 80202

Dear Records Administrator,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City and County of Denver's use of generative AI tools by city employees, including Microsoft Copilot Chat and Ask Dee (AiseraGPT).

Pursuant to the Colorado Open Records Act, C.R.S. § 24-72-203(3)(b), we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from Ask Dee (AiseraGPT), Microsoft Copilot Chat, Sunny AI, ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City and County of Denver employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City and County of Denver employee use of Ask Dee, Microsoft Copilot Chat, Sunny AI, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The City and County of Denver has deployed Ask Dee, powered by AiseraGPT, which uses domain-specific large language models to automatically resolve employee IT support requests such as software provisioning, account troubleshooting, and hardware issues, auto-resolving 81% of inquiries (see Aisera, "City & County of Denver Customer Story," https://aisera.com/customers/city-and-county-of-denver/). Denver's Chief AI and Information Officer Suma Nallapati has overseen the development of the Sunny AI platform and the city's partnership with Microsoft on Copilot Chat for employee use (see Harvard Data-Smart City Solutions, "Personalizing Government at Scale: Denver's AI Strategy," https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/personalizing-denver-ai; see also GovTech, "How a New Title for Denver's CIO Helps Power AI Work," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/how-a-new-title-for-denvers-cio-helps-power-ai-work). Denver has also issued an RFP seeking skilled AI vendors to drive innovation and improve the resident experience (see City and County of Denver, "AI RFP Press Release," https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Technology-Services/News/AI-RFP-Press-Release).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Ask Dee (AiseraGPT) maintains server-side conversation logs and resolution data accessible through the Aisera administrative dashboard. For Microsoft Copilot Chat, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Colorado has enacted one of the nation's most comprehensive AI records mandates. The Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205, signed 2024, effective February 1, 2026) requires deployers of high-risk AI systems — including government agencies — to implement risk management policies, conduct impact assessments, and maintain documentation and records of AI system use and outcomes. C.R.S. § 6-1-1701 et seq. The Colorado Open Records Act defines "public records" to include all writings made or maintained by any state agency regardless of format. C.R.S. § 24-72-202. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Public Records Officer
North Carolina Department of Information Technology
4101 Mail Service Center
Raleigh, NC 27699-4101

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to North Carolina's use of generative AI tools by state employees, including Microsoft 365 Copilot and ChatGPT Enterprise.

We are requesting the following records under N.C.G.S. § 132-6(a):

1. All interaction logs from Microsoft 365 Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of Microsoft 365 Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for Microsoft 365 Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, and other generative AI tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

North Carolina has been a national leader in AI governance. Governor Stein signed Executive Order No. 24 establishing statewide principles, governance, and accountability for AI use, and NCDIT appointed the state's first Deputy Secretary for AI and Policy (see Governor Josh Stein, "Executive Order No. 24: Advancing Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence," Sept. 2, 2025, https://governor.nc.gov/executive-order-no-24-advancing-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence-benefits-all-north-carolinians; see also NCDIT, "2025: A Landmark Year for NCDIT," Jan. 12, 2026, https://it.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/01/12/2025-landmark-year-ncdit-ai-innovation-connectivity-and-security-advancements). The N.C. Department of State Treasurer conducted a 12-week ChatGPT Enterprise pilot with 36 employees in partnership with OpenAI, with 85% reporting a positive experience (see GovTech, "North Carolina Hacks the Treasury Bureaucracy With ChatGPT," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/north-carolina-hacks-the-treasury-bureaucracy-with-chatgpt; see also NC Treasurer, "Lessons from NC Treasurer's Office Generative AI Pilot with ChatGPT," https://www.nctreasurer.gov/nccunc-treasurer-ai-survey-report/open). NCDIT has introduced an AI User Code of Conduct and established an AI Accelerator as a centralized hub for AI governance.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. North Carolina's Public Records Act defines "public record" to include all documentary material regardless of physical form or characteristics, including "electronic data-processing records," made or received in connection with official business. N.C.G.S. § 132-1. Governor Stein's Executive Order No. 31 (2025) directed agencies to inventory AI uses and develop responsible AI policies. The NC Department of Information Technology has issued AI use policies requiring documentation of AI tools deployed, human oversight of AI-assisted decisions, and retention of AI-generated materials as public records. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Governor Youngkin signed Executive Order 30, and VITA subsequently published the "Utilization of Artificial Intelligence by the Commonwealth of Virginia" policy standard governing AI use across state agencies (see https://www.vita.virginia.gov/media/vitavirginiagov/it-governance/ea/pdf/Utilization-of-Artificial-Intelligence-by-COV-Policy-Standard.pdf). VITA's AI registry has approved 112 AI use cases submitted by state agencies (see Virginia Mercury, "Virginia's artificial intelligence registry faces transparency challenges, study shows," Nov. 12, 2025, https://virginiamercury.com/2025/11/12/virginias-artificial-intelligence-registry-faces-transparency-challenges-study-shows/). Copilot Chat and the AI translation tool Smartling have been made available to all executive branch agencies, with VITA offering virtual training sessions on Copilot Chat for Commonwealth employees (see https://www.vita.virginia.gov/news--events/latest-events/name-1057122-en.html).

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Virginia Freedom of Information Act, Va. Code 2.2-3700 et seq., public records include "all writings and recordings that consist of letters, words or numbers, or their equivalent, set down by handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostatting, photography, magnetic impulse, optical or magneto-optical form, mechanical or electronic recording or other form of data compilation, however stored, and regardless of physical form or characteristics, prepared or owned by, or in the possession of a public body or its officers, employees or agents in the transaction of public business." Va. Code 2.2-3701. AI chatbot interaction logs used by state employees in their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Executive Order 30 and VITA's AI policy further confirm that these AI tools are being deployed as part of official state operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
City of Tucson
255 W. Alameda Street
Tucson, AZ 85701

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City of Tucson's use of generative AI tools by city employees, including Axon Draft One and generative AI tools used for permit processing.

Pursuant to the Arizona Public Records Law, A.R.S. § 39-121.01(D)(1), we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from Axon Draft One, ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City of Tucson employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City of Tucson employee use of Axon Draft One, generative AI permit-processing tools, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Tucson's public safety teams are piloting Axon's Draft One, which leverages generative AI and body-worn camera audio to produce draft police report narratives, with deputies reporting approximately one hour saved each day (see CNN, "How AI is being used by police departments to help draft reports," Aug. 12, 2025, https://www.cnn.com/2025/08/12/tech/ai-police-reports-axon; see also Axon, "Draft One," https://www.axon.com/products/draft-one). Administrative staff also use generative and predictive AI tools to speed up permits, translations, and data analysis. The City's Advanced Technology Committee and Technology & Data Policies require supplemental reviews, human oversight, bias checks, and director signoff before deploying AI systems (see Nucamp, "The Complete Guide to Using AI in the Government Industry in Tucson in 2025," https://www.nucamp.co/blog/coding-bootcamp-tucson-az-government-the-complete-guide-to-using-ai-in-the-government-industry-in-tucson-in-2025).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Axon Draft One generates draft police reports from body-worn camera audio; the initial AI-generated drafts and any officer edits may be retained in the Axon Evidence system. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Arizona's public records law requires that public bodies promptly furnish copies of public records to any person requesting them. A.R.S. § 39-121.01(D)(1). Arizona courts have broadly interpreted "public records" to include electronic communications and digital records created in the transaction of public business. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
San Francisco Department of Technology
One South Van Ness Avenue, 2nd Floor
San Francisco, CA 94103

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to San Francisco's use of generative AI tools by city employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat, ChatGPT, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by city employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing city employee use of Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat, ChatGPT, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat and any other generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

On July 14, 2025, Mayor Daniel Lurie announced that Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat, powered by OpenAI's GPT-4o, would be made available to nearly 30,000 city employees, making San Francisco the largest U.S. city to offer generative AI to its entire workforce (see CNBC, "San Francisco rolls out Microsoft's Copilot AI for 30,000 city workers," July 14, 2025, https://www.cnbc.com/2025/07/14/san-francisco-ai.html; see also SF.gov, "Mayor Lurie Brings AI Technology to San Francisco Government," https://www.sf.gov/news-mayor-lurie-brings-ai-technology-to-san-francisco-government-cementing-city-as-global-ai-leader). The rollout followed a successful six-month AI pilot in which more than 2,000 city employees tested generative AI tools and reported productivity gains of up to five hours per week (see GovTech, "San Francisco Gives Thousands of City Staffers AI Access," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/san-francisco-gives-thousands-of-city-staffers-ai-access). The Department of Technology launched a five-week training program with live workshops and office hours for Copilot Chat.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that for Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. Given the scale of the Copilot Chat deployment (30,000 employees), Microsoft's enterprise administrative tools should enable bulk export of interaction data. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the California Public Records Act, Cal. Gov. Code 6253(c), and the San Francisco Sunshine Ordinance, public records include "any writing containing information relating to the conduct of the public's business prepared, owned, used, or retained by any state or local agency regardless of physical form or characteristics." Cal. Gov. Code 6252(e). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by city employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. The city's official announcement of the Copilot Chat rollout to all 30,000 employees confirms these tools are part of the conduct of public business. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

Alexander Spangher

Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

Pennsylvania conducted a first-in-the-nation pilot program in partnership with OpenAI, in which 175 employees from 14 agencies used ChatGPT Enterprise over the course of a year, reporting an average time savings of 95 minutes per day (see Shapiro Administration, "Lessons from Pennsylvania's Generative AI Pilot with ChatGPT," https://www.pa.gov/content/dam/copapwp-pagov/en/oa/documents/programs/information-technology/documents/openai-pilot-report-2025.pdf; see also City & State PA, "Shapiro says AI has 'real promise' as he unveils pilot program results," Mar. 2025, https://www.cityandstatepa.com/policy/2025/03/shapiro-says-ai-has-real-promise-he-unveils-pilot-program-results/403965/). Over 1,300 employees have completed InnovateUS training on safe AI use, with 3,200 more enrolled (see Spotlight PA, "Critics warn of risks as more PA governments turn to AI," Aug. 2025, https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/08/ai-chatgpt-shapiro-pennsylvania-government-environment/; see also PublicSource, "Pennsylvania government to expand employees' use of AI," https://www.publicsource.org/pennsylvania-government-expand-artificial-intelligence-ai/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Pennsylvania's Right-to-Know Law defines "record" as information regardless of physical form that documents a transaction or activity of an agency. 65 P.S. § 67.102. The Office of Open Records has confirmed that electronic communications on all platforms constitute public records. Governor Shapiro's Executive Order 2024-02 established a Generative AI Governance Board, and the Office of Information Technology issued GenAI guidelines directing state employees to document AI tool usage and treat AI-generated outputs as agency records. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

The county published a comprehensive AI Roadmap identifying high-priority AI projects, including an AI assistant for public benefits applications and a human-resources chatbot for employee benefits questions (see Miami-Dade County, "Artificial Intelligence (AI) Roadmap," https://www.miamidade.gov/ai/; see also Miami-Dade County, "Artificial Intelligence Report 2025," https://www.miamidade.gov/technology/library/artificial-intelligence-report-2025.pdf). The county has deployed the Strive 305 Bot, an AI assistant with information on starting a business and working with the county, and is building toward more sophisticated AI assistants that provide a "No Wrong Door" government experience across multiple departments (see Miami-Dade County, "The Vision for 2026 and the Future," https://www.miamidade.gov/global/comms-tech/technology/ai/ai-vision-for-2026-and-future.page). Miami-Dade also launched Microsoft Copilot Chat for county employees in June 2025 (see Miami-Dade County, "Microsoft Copilot Chat launches for County employees," https://www.miamidade.gov/global/news-item.page?Mduid_news=news1751026662194812; see also GovTech, "Miami-Dade County, Fla., Works to Put AI in Action," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/miami-dade-county-fla-works-to-put-ai-in-action).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Microsoft Copilot Chat interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. Copilot usage reports and activity data can be accessed via the Microsoft 365 admin center under Reports > Usage. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. AI chatbot platforms such as Strive 305 Bot typically maintain server-side conversation logs accessible through their administrative dashboards. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Florida has one of the nation's broadest public records laws, defining "public records" as all materials "regardless of the physical form, characteristics, or means of transmission, made or received pursuant to law or ordinance or in connection with the transaction of official business." Fla. Stat. § 119.011(12). The Attorney General has opined that electronic communications on any platform are public records. Miami-Dade County has published an AI Roadmap (miamidade.gov/ai) and ranked #1 nationally in county AI adoption, reflecting extensive AI deployment that generates records subject to Chapter 119. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
Executive Office of Technology Services and Security
Commonwealth of Massachusetts
One Ashburton Place
Boston, MA 02108

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

1. All interaction logs from the ChatGPT Enterprise AI Assistant, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from February 1, 2026 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of the ChatGPT Enterprise AI Assistant or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for the ChatGPT Enterprise AI Assistant showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Governor Healey announced in February 2026 that Massachusetts would become the first state to deploy ChatGPT across its entire executive branch, making the tool available to nearly 40,000 state employees in a secure, walled-off environment through a three-year contract with OpenAI (see Governor Healey Announces Massachusetts to Become First State to Deploy ChatGPT Across Executive Branch, Mass.gov, Feb. 2026, https://www.mass.gov/news/governor-healey-announces-massachusetts-to-become-first-state-to-deploy-chatgpt-across-executive-branch; see also Boston Globe, "ChatGPT-powered AI assistant to help Massachusetts state workers," Feb. 12, 2026, https://www.bostonglobe.com/2026/02/12/business/chatgpt-ai-massachusetts-state-workers-healey/). The rollout began with staff in the Executive Office of Technology Services and Security, with additional secretariats being phased in (see StateScoop, "Massachusetts debuts ChatGPT-powered AI assistant for all executive-branch employees," https://statescoop.com/massachusetts-chatgpt-ai-executive-branch/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Massachusetts defines "public records" broadly to include all documentary materials regardless of physical form, explicitly covering electronic communications and digital records. M.G.L. c. 4, § 7(26). The Executive Office of Technology Services and Security (EOTSS) has issued guidelines requiring that AI-generated outputs used in official business be documented and retained. Governor Healey's Executive Order No. 629 (2023) directed state agencies to ensure transparency and accountability in AI use. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

The Maryland General Assembly subsequently enacted the AI Governance Act of 2024 (SB 818), providing statutory backing for AI oversight and reporting across state agencies (see https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Legislation/Details/SB0818?ys=2024rs). Fifty-nine state agencies are now using Google Workspace with Gemini, providing nearly 43,000 state employees access to Gemini's AI tools, with 12,500 active users already testing its capabilities (see GovTech, "Maryland Expands AI Integration Across State Government," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/maryland-expands-ai-integration-across-state-government). Maryland has also piloted Microsoft Copilot for state employee use.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, workspace administrators may access usage logs through the Google Admin console. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Maryland Public Information Act, Md. State Gov't 10-611 et seq., public records include "the original or any copy of any documentary material that is made by a unit or instrumentality of the State government or of a political subdivision, is received by a unit or instrumentality of the State government or of a political subdivision in connection with the transaction of public business, and is in any form." Md. State Gov't 10-611(g)(1). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by state employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Executive Order 01.01.2024.02 and the AI Governance Act of 2024 (SB 818) further establish that AI tools are part of official state government operations subject to oversight and reporting. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

communications.subject: Public Information Act Request :: R304156-041626
communications.communication:
Dear Alexander Spangher: 
Thank you for your interest in public records of City of Houston. Your request has been received and is being processed in accordance with Chapter 552 of Texas Government Code, the Public Information Act. Your request was received in this office on 4/16/2026 and given the reference number R304156-041626 for tracking purposes. Records Requested: Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs 

April 9, 2026 

Public Information Officer 
City of Houston 
P.O. Box 1562 
Houston, TX 77251 

Dear Public Information Officer, 

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work. 

We are writing to request records related to the City of Houston's use of generative AI tools by city employees. 

Pursuant to the Texas Public Information Act, Tex. Gov't Code § 552.221, we are requesting the following records: 

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City of Houston employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV). 

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City of Houston employee use of generative AI chatbot tools. 

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data. 

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees. 

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses). 

Houston CIO Lisa Kent has discussed the City's "AI Wins" and broader technology vision for the city's nearly 22,000 employees across 23 departments (see Tech Tables Podcast, Episode 221, "Lisa Kent, Melissa Kraft, Derek Williams — 2026 World Cup, Fusion Teams, AI Wins, Leading Well," https://techtables.com/podcast/221-lisa-kent-melissa-kraft-derek-williams-2026-world-cup-fusion-teams-ai-wins-leading-well). The City's Houston Information Technology Services (HITS) division has focused on leveraging technology to enhance the citizen experience and boost efficiencies (see https://www.houstontx.gov/hits/chief_information_officer.html). 

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files. 

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20. 

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below. 

Thank you for your time and assistance. 

Sincerely, 
Alexander Spangher 
Postdoctoral Researcher 
Stanford University 
alexspan@stanford.edu 

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, administrators can access interaction logs through the Google Workspace Admin Console under Reporting > Audit and Investigation. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful. 

P.P.S. The Texas Public Information Act defines "public information" as information collected, assembled, or maintained in connection with official business by a governmental body, including electronic data. Tex. Gov't Code § 552.002. The Texas Supreme Court confirmed in City of Dallas v. Abbott that text messages and electronic communications are public information. The Department of Information Resources issued Generative AI Guidelines (2024) recommending that agencies document AI tool usage and treat AI-generated outputs as public information. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2266843/ 
Your request will be forwarded to the relevant City of Houston department(s) to locate the information you seek and to determine the volume and any costs associated with satisfying your request. You will be contacted about the availability and/or provided with copies of the records in question. PLEASE NOTE: The Chapter 552 of Texas Government Code, the Public Information Act does not require a governmental body to create new information, to do legal research, or to answer questions. 
 
You can monitor the progress of your request at the link below and you'll receive an email when your request has been completed. Again, thank you for using the Houston Public Information Request Center. 
City of Houston

communications.communication:
Subject: FOIA Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 7, 2026

Office of Information Policy
U.S. Department of Justice
441 G Street NW, 6th Floor
Washington, DC 20530
DOJ.OIP.FOIA@usdoj.gov

Dear FOIA Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the Department of Justice's use of generative AI tools by department employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from the OIG internal Azure OpenAI chatbot (DOJ-0195), DEA policy query chatbot (DOJ-0316), FBI policy chatbot (DOJ-0269), ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by DOJ headquarters employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing DOJ employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

DOJ's 2025 AI Use Case Inventory documents 315 AI use cases — up from 241 in 2024 and 15 in 2023 — including multiple component-level GenAI chatbots across OIG, DEA, FBI, EOUSA, and BOP (downloadable at https://www.justice.gov/ai/ai-inventory; see also FedScoop, Feb 11, 2026: https://fedscoop.com/justice-department-artificial-intelligence-ai-surveillance-inventory-predictive-technology-algorithm-bias/). The Bureau of Prisons has used ChatGPT to "improve the quality of written communication, emails, and fliers." A department-wide Microsoft Copilot deployment is also underway. Dillon Bergin of MuckRock filed related FOIA requests to OIP in January 2025, and immigration attorney Matthew Hoppock obtained DOJ's internal AI guidance via FOIA.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Claude, export is similar, except via "Settings > Privacy > Export Data". For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. See "Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA," ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025), https://www.executivegov.com/articles/army-garciga-application-owners-ai-records-mgmt.

The office's model-res-avm uses machine learning trained on property characteristics and sales data to generate assessed values for all residential properties (see GitHub, Cook County Assessor's Office, https://github.com/ccao-data). The Assessor's Office also released its 2025 Open Data Refresh with newly available data assets (see Cook County Open Data, "Assessor — 2025 Open Data Refresh," https://datacatalog.cookcountyil.gov/stories/s/Assessor-2025-Open-Data-Refresh/gzdr-q7c4/).

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, administrators can access interaction logs through the Google Workspace Admin Console under Reporting > Audit and Investigation. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Illinois's Freedom of Information Act explicitly enumerates "electronic data processing records, electronic communications, and recorded information of any kind" as public records. 5 ILCS 140/2(c). The Public Access Counselor within the AG's office has issued binding opinions confirming that electronic messages are public records. Governor Pritzker's Executive Order 2024-07 directed the creation of an AI governance framework including documentation requirements. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. title: Public Records Request — Connecticut Generative AI Records

communications.subject: Connecticut Freedom of Information Act Request: Public Records Request — Connecticut Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Records Officer
Bureau of Information Technology Solutions
Connecticut Department of Administrative Services
450 Columbus Boulevard
Hartford, CT 06103

Dear Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Connecticut's use of generative AI tools by state employees, including ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot.

We are requesting the following records under Conn. Gen. Stat. § 1-206(a):

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or other generative AI tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Connecticut has conducted both a ChatGPT pilot and a Microsoft M365 Copilot trial, with over 260 employees taking specialized public sector AI training and engaging in experiential learning through these tools (see NASCIO Awards Submission, "State of Connecticut: Bureau of Information Technology," https://www.nascio.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/CT_Artificial-Intelligence.pdf). The state has also established an AI Enablement Lab for safe experimentation with AI technologies and maintains an annual AI inventory required by Public Act 23-16 (see Connecticut 2025 State AI Inventory, https://portal.ct.gov/das/knowledge-base/articles/bits/ai-inventory). Connecticut's Responsible AI Framework, released February 2024, provides governance guidelines for AI deployment across state agencies (see Connecticut Responsible AI Policy Framework, https://portal.ct.gov/-/media/OPM/Fin-General/Policies/CT-Responsible-AI-Policy-Framework-Final-02012024.pdf).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Connecticut's Freedom of Information Act defines "public records" as any recorded data or information relating to the conduct of the public's business, including electronic data. Conn. Gen. Stat. § 1-200(5). Public Act 24-130 (2024) requires state agencies to complete an inventory of all AI systems used in consequential decision-making, including documentation of purpose, data inputs, and outcomes. Governor Lamont's Executive Order No. 1 (2023) directed the development of an AI policy framework emphasizing transparency and accountability. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

communications.subject: Nevada Public Records Act Request: Public Records Request — ClarkCounty Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
Clark County
500 S. Grand Central Parkway
Las Vegas, NV 89155

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Clark County's use of generative AI tools by county employees, including the county's AI pilots and AI agents.

Pursuant to the Nevada Public Records Act, NRS § 239.0107, we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from any AI agent or chatbot tools, ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by Clark County employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing Clark County employee use of AI agents or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to county employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Clark County is running 15 to 20 AI pilots simultaneously and has placed AI agents directly on its organizational chart alongside human employees, reflecting one of the most ambitious local government AI strategies in the nation. CIO Bob Leek oversees technology for 2.3 million residents and 50 million annual tourists, managing enterprise infrastructure including Harry Reid International Airport. The county has reframed AI as "augmented intelligence" emphasizing human-AI collaboration, and has deployed website translation across 30+ languages with real-time translation pilots underway (see Tech Tables Podcast, "#214: Clark County: $370M Budget + 4M Daily Cyberattacks + AI Agents on Org Chart (& Running 20 AI Pilots)," https://techtables.com/podcast/214-clark-county-370m-budget-4m-daily-cyberattacks-ai-agents-on-org-chart-running-20-ai-pilots; see also Infosecurity Magazine, "Clark County CIO Talks Securing AI Innovation and Broadband Initiative," https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/interviews/clark-county-cio-cybersecurity/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that AI agent platforms typically maintain server-side conversation and interaction logs accessible through their administrative dashboards. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, administrators can access interaction logs through the Google Workspace Admin Console under Reporting > Audit and Investigation. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Nevada's public records law provides that all public books and records of a governmental entity must be open to inspection by any person. NRS § 239.010. The statute covers records "regardless of the form or medium" including electronic data. Clark County has placed AI agents formally on its organizational chart as "virtual employees," reflecting the integration of AI tools into official county operations and the expectation that interactions with these tools generate public records. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

alexspan@stanford.edu

AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by city employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition.
