Dear Alexander Spangher, Thank you for your interest in public records of City of Boston. Your request has been received and is being processed. Your request was received in this office on 4/16/2026 10:29:38 PM and given the reference number R002129-041626 for tracking purposes. Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs 

April 9, 2026 

Public Records Officer 
City of Boston 
1 City Hall Square 
Boston, MA 02201 

Dear Public Records Officer, 

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work. 

We are writing to request records related to Boston's use of generative AI tools by city employees. 

We are requesting the following records: 

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by city employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV). 

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing city employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or other generative AI chatbot tools. 

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data. 

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed. 

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses). 

Boston was among the first major U.S. cities to issue generative AI guidelines for employees in May 2023 (see https://www.boston.gov/sites/default/files/file/2023/05/Guidelines-for-Using-Generative-AI-2023.pdf). A spring 2025 citywide survey found that about 6 in 10 respondents have tried generative AI, with 1 in 4 using it weekly at work, and 78% expressed interest in learning how to use generative AI for their work (see Boston.gov, "Ensuring the Responsible Public Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence," https://www.boston.gov/news/ensuring-responsible-public-use-generative-artificial-intelligence). The city has also deployed Google Gemini-powered AI search on the 60 most visited pages on boston.gov and has published a list of AI-related projects underway across city departments (see https://www.boston.gov/government/cabinets/innovation-and-technology/ai-related-projects-city-boston). Harvard's Data-Smart City Solutions has profiled Boston's approach as a model for government AI adoption (see https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/generative-ai-and-possibility-government). 

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files. 

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20. 

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below. 

Thank you for your time and assistance. 

Sincerely, 
Alexander Spangher 
Postdoctoral Researcher 
Stanford University 
alexspan@stanford.edu 

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, workspace administrators may access usage logs through the Google Admin console. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful. 

P.P.S. Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law, M.G.L. c.66, 10(b), every person has a right to access public records. "Public records" are defined broadly under M.G.L. c.4, 7(26) to include "all books, papers, maps, photographs, recorded tapes, financial statements, statistical tabulations, or other documentary materials or data, regardless of physical form or characteristics, made or received by any officer or employee of any agency." AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by city employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Boston's published Interim Guidelines for Using Generative AI confirm that these tools are part of the conduct of public business. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records. 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2266835/ You can monitor the progress of your request at the link below and you'll receive an email when your request has been completed. Again, thank you for using the Public Records Center. Director of Public Records 
Records Access Officer 
City of Boston

The city currently uses AI for social media posts and an AI chatbot for its 311 service, and police departments across Georgia and the nation have adopted AI tools such as Axon's Draft One to generate draft police reports from body-worn camera audio (see CNN, "How AI is being used by police departments to help draft reports," Aug. 12, 2025, https://www.cnn.com/2025/08/12/tech/ai-police-reports-axon; see also EFF, "AI Police Reports: Year In Review," Dec. 2025, https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2025/12/ai-police-reports-year-review).

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Axon Draft One generates draft police reports from body-worn camera audio; the initial AI-generated drafts and any officer edits may be retained in the Axon Evidence system. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Georgia's Open Records Act defines "public record" to include all documents and "computer-based or generated information" prepared, maintained, or received by an agency in the course of operations. O.C.G.A. § 50-18-70(a). Governor Kemp's Executive Order 07.25.23.02 (2023) created a Commission on AI in state government, and the Georgia Technology Authority has issued AI use guidance recommending documentation of AI deployments and treatment of AI-generated work product as public records. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by city employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing city employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or other generative AI chatbot tools, including but not limited to agency-specific AI use approvals.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed, broken down by agency where available.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Stanford University

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed. 

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses). 

The Indiana Secretary of State's Office launched "Captain Record," a generative AI tool built on Google Cloud's Vertex AI and Gemini 2.0, enabling users to search more than 100 years of state documents using natural language queries (see StateScoop, "Indiana launches 'Captain Record,' the AI tool that searches 100 years of state documents," https://statescoop.com/indiana-captain-record-ai-search-tool/). The state also launched the "Ask Indiana" public-facing AI chatbot on IN.gov (see Indiana Capital Chronicle, "Meet 'Ask Indiana,' state government's new AI chatbot," May 15, 2025, https://indianacapitalchronicle.com/2025/05/15/meet-ask-indiana-state-governments-new-ai-resident-assistant-chatbot/). Additionally, the Secretary of State's Office partnered with Google to launch "Liz" and "Otto," AI chatbots supporting the INBiz platform and auto dealer licensing system, respectively. Indiana's AI Policy and Guidance is published at https://www.in.gov/mph/AI/. 

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files. 

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20. 

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below. 

Thank you for your time and assistance. 

Sincerely, 
Alexander Spangher 
Postdoctoral Researcher 
Stanford University 
alexspan@stanford.edu 

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Google Gemini-based tools such as "Captain Record," logs may be available through Google Cloud administrative consoles or server-side conversation storage. For "Ask Indiana," logs may be available through the platform's administration tools. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful. 

P.P.S. Under Indiana's Access to Public Records Act, IC 5-14-3, public records include "any writing, paper, report, study, map, photograph, book, card, tape recording, or other material that is created, received, retained, maintained, or filed by or with a public agency." IC 5-14-3-2(q). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by state employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Indiana's published AI Policy and Guidance further confirms that these AI tools are part of official state operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records. 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2266805/" 
This message acknowledges that your request has been received and is being processed in accordance with the Indiana Access to Public Records Act, under Ind. Code 5-14-3, et seq. (“APRA”).  Your request was received on April 17, 2026 and given the reference number S042942-041726 for tracking purposes. 
Your request will be reviewed and processed by the City/County agency you’ve selected to determine: 
(1) if the request identifies the public records you seek with “reasonable particularity” (in accordance with Ind. Code 5-14-3-3(a)); 
(2) if the agency has any public records that are responsive to your request; AND 
(3) whether the responsive public records are disclosable to you (in accordance with Ind. Code 5-14-3-4). 
APRA requires City/County agencies to produce responsive and disclosable public records (should they exist) within a reasonable time based upon the circumstances.  If any responsive public records are not disclosable to you, the agency will remove the non-disclosable portion from the public record and provide the statutory basis for any such denial.  Information pertaining to any copying fees (if applicable) will also be provided. 
NOTE: please understand that the City/County receives a large number of APRA requests, and particular types of public records may take longer to retrieve and review than others.  In many instances, an agency will rely upon our Information Services Agency to assist in retrieval of public records (like emails and other electronic messages/information).  Further, each responsive document must be reviewed by the agency to determine whether it is disclosable (pursuant to Ind. Code 5-14-3-4).  Depending on the size and complexity of the request, the determination as to whether a record is disclosable may take additional time. 
While there is no fixed due date for providing the records, if more than thirty (30) days has passed since your last communication with the agency, feel free to contact the City/County Public Access Counselor (317-327-4055; pac@indy.gov) for assistance. 
Thank you, 
The Consolidated City of Indianapolis and Marion County.

Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
Washington Technology Solutions (WaTech)
P.O. Box 43113
Olympia, WA 98504-3113

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Washington State's use of generative AI tools by state employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Governor Inslee issued Executive Order 24-01 on January 30, 2024, directing agencies to develop policies on the use of generative AI and to assess impacts on the state workforce (see https://governor.wa.gov/sites/default/files/exe_order/24-01%20-%20Artificial%20Intelligence%20(tmp).pdf). WaTech subsequently developed generative AI guidelines for state agencies, and agencies have been adopting ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot for employee use (see KNKX Public Radio, "How local governments in Washington state are using ChatGPT," Aug. 26, 2025, https://www.knkx.org/government/2025-08-26/washington-city-officials-chatgpt-write-government-documents-artificial-intelligence; see also OPB, "As Washington state government officials embrace AI, policies are still catching up," Aug. 31, 2025, https://www.opb.org/article/2025/08/31/washington-government-officials-embrace-ai/). WaTech also published a State of Washington Generative AI Report in October 2024 and developed a Generative AI Accountability Framework under EO 24-01.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under Washington's Public Records Act, RCW 42.56, public records include "any writing containing information relating to the conduct of government or the performance of any governmental or proprietary function prepared, owned, used, or retained by any state or local agency." RCW 42.56.010(3). AI chatbot interaction logs used by state employees in their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. We also note that Executive Order 24-01 specifically directed WaTech to develop policies for state agencies' use of generative AI, underscoring that these interactions are part of official government business. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

New Hampshire Department of Information Technology
21 South Fruit Street, Suite 16
Concord, NH 03301

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

According to a July 1, 2025 report by New Hampshire Department of Information Technology Commissioner Denis Goulet, the New Hampshire Department of Justice uses Lexis+ AI to streamline legal research for its attorneys, and the New Hampshire Veterans Home uses ChatGPT for professional letters, communications, and client notes. Other departments developing or exploring AI frameworks include the Department of Information Technology, the Department of Administrative Services, New Hampshire Employment Security, and the New Hampshire Banking Department (see New Hampshire Bulletin, "Meeting with developers, New Hampshire representatives assess AI tools, concerns," Dec. 18, 2025, https://newhampshirebulletin.com/2025/12/18/meeting-with-developers-representatives-assess-ai-tools-concerns/; see also Valley News, "AI in New Hampshire: Benefits and Regulatory Concerns," Dec. 21, 2025, https://vnews.com/2025/12/21/ai-state-government-adoption/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. New Hampshire's Right-to-Know Law covers all "governmental records," defined as any information created, accepted, or obtained by any public body in furtherance of its official function. RSA 91-A:1-a, III. The NH Supreme Court has confirmed that electronic communications are governmental records subject to disclosure. The Department of Information Technology has issued preliminary guidance on AI tool use by state employees recommending documentation of AI-generated work product and adherence to existing records retention schedules. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

The Wisconsin Department of Safety and Professional Services (DSPS) deployed the Maverick AI platform by Google Cloud and MTX to automate data entry and identify bottlenecks in the licensing process, using AI models trained on DSPS license applications (see Route Fifty, "Wisconsin looks to AI, cloud to improve professional licensing process," https://www.route-fifty.com/infrastructure/2021/10/wisconsin-looks-to-ai-cloud-to-improve-professional-licensing-process/316456/). DWD also announced advancements in unemployment insurance modernization in collaboration with Google Public Sector (see DWD, Sept. 3, 2024, https://dwd.wisconsin.gov/press/2024/240903-ui-modernization.htm). The Governor's Task Force on Workforce and Artificial Intelligence has examined how AI is reshaping Wisconsin's businesses and job market (see DWD, "Governor's Task Force on Workforce and Artificial Intelligence," https://dwd.wisconsin.gov/ai-taskforce/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Wisconsin's Public Records Law defines "record" as any material on which information is recorded or preserved, regardless of physical form or characteristics, which has been created or is being kept by an authority. Wis. Stat. § 19.32(2). The Wisconsin Supreme Court in Schill v. Wisconsin Rapids School District (2010) confirmed that text messages are public records. Governor Evers's Executive Order #225 (2024) directed state agencies to develop responsible AI use guidelines, and the Division of Enterprise Technology has issued AI guidelines directing agencies to document generative AI tool usage and treat AI-generated work product as records under the Public Records Law. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.
